other_observers: Norm Erthal, Glenn Walbek, Ira Sanders were all with me on July 12th.; Susan Allerton of Durango found the bird and called Jim Beatty who confirmed it. Both took some poor photos.  Dick Schottler and Joe Roller saw this bird the following morning (July 13th). Nathan Pieplow saw this bird in the evening
description: Small hummingbird about the size of nearby Broad-tailed Hummingbirds. Back mostly blue-green with faint buffy edges to the lower back and uppertail coverts. The crown was dark gray. The lores and auriculars were black and was bordered on top by a very wide white supercillium. The throat whitish with small green "flecks" becoming larger and more abundant on the chest and sides. Center of belly less "flecked". Undertail coverts appeared white. Wings gray and primary length was even with the tail. Tail was blue-green. Eyes black. Bill mostly black with just a small flesh-colored base to the lower mandible (seen in some photos).Behaviors: This bird was seen daily from 6am-8:30am and again in the evenings from about 5 or 6pm till 8:30pm.  On July 12th when I observed the bird it would show up and feed for about 30 seconds every 10 minutes. The times between feedings got longer and longer (it was 30 minutes at 8:30am when I left).  There were many Rufous (about 75), Broad-tailed (about 100), Black-chinned (about 30) and Calliope (about 3) hummingbirds all feeding at various times. Like the other hummingbirds this White-eared would fly in and patiently wait for a perch to open on the left most (western most) of five feeders placed in a row on the deck of Jim and Kim Fisher of Durango. No interactions between the White-eared and others were witnessed.Call: None heardPlumage: Adult
photographer: Photographer: Joey M Kellner, Joey M Kellner, Joey M Kellner, Joey M Kellner
